text: Hello everyone, this is a video vaccine demonstrating the feasibility of laparoscopic approach for beyond TME rectal resection. Our patient is a fifty-three years lady with a growth at six centimeters from the anal verge, poorly differentiated mucinous adenocarcinoma, non-metastatic on staging worker. The MRI here shows the growth in middle third rectum infiltrating the uterine body and disease close to the right ureter. She received long course cape cytobine radiotherapy, fifty point four gray. Response assessment MRI scan was done at six weeks, which shows partial response with disease abutting the uterine body and the disease being still close to the right vesico-uretic junction. She was planned for laparoscopic low anterior rectal resection with type III radical hysterectomy with right pelvic lymph node resection.

The surgeon performs posterior pelvic dissection in the TME plane, followed by right lateral dissection, ligating the uterine artery and continuing ureteric dissection to the vesico-uretic junction. The left side dissection mirrors the right, leading to anterior dissection, colpotomy, rectal stapling, and colorectal anastomosis using the Knight-Griffin technique. The posterior dissection safeguards hypogastric nerves down to the levator and IM flow. Lateral dissection on the right involves traction on the broad ligament, dissection around the round ligament, proceeding along external iliac vessels to the infundibulopelvic ligament and ureter. The infundibulopelvic ligament is dissected, clipped, and transected using an energy device, with careful dissection over the external iliac vessels to avoid injury.

The surgeon continues the ureteric dissection, leading to the medialization of the ureters, identifying the ureter at its crossing over the common iliac artery. The dissection proceeds until the crossing of the uterine artery over the ureter is identified. The assistant changes traction, facilitating the resection of the obliterated umbilical artery, which is dissected from its origin at the internal iliac artery to the point where the uterine artery originates. Dissection continues along the uterine artery within the medial paravasical space, bounded by the ureter medially, the uterine vessel laterally, and the pelvic floor inferiorly. Attention then shifts to the lateral paravasical space and the obturator fossa.

The surgeon continues resection along the surface of the external iliac vessels, removing fibrofatty tissue from the lateral pelvic wall and superiorly from the perivesical fat. Tributaries of the external iliac vein are secured. The fibrofatty tissue, containing lymph nodes, is demarcated from the yellowish perivesical fat and removed from the obturator fossa until the obturator nerve is identified. Dissection clears lymphoidal tissue from the lateral paravesical space, bounded laterally by the external iliac vessels and medially by branches of the internal iliac artery. A bipolar device is used to prevent thermal injury to the obturator nerve. The obturator artery is dissected, clipped, and ligated to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon removes the entire lymph canal packet and places it in a specimen retrieval bag. The lateral dissection continues with the uterine artery being dissected and clipped using ligaclips and an energy device. The ureter is carefully separated from the stump of the uterine artery, with dissection extending to the uretrovesical junction due to suspected proximity of disease to the right ureter. An energy device combined with cold scissors is used to meticulously dissect the ureteric pedicle, ensuring protection against thermal injury.

The surgeon resects the ureter up to the vesico-ureteric junction, ensuring it is mobilized away from the stump of the uterine artery. Further dissection reveals the deep uterine vein, which is meticulously clipped to prevent bleeding. The right hypogastric nerve is visible near the uterus and is carefully dissected and clipped at the pelvic floor level, beyond the branches supplying the rectum. Remaining attachments are removed, completing the lateral dissection.

The surgeon dissects the vesico-ureteric junction from the uterus and vagina, preparing for colpotomy. The paravascular and pararectal spaces are clearly visible after the lateral dissection. The rectum is turned to the right to facilitate lateral dissection on the left side, focusing on the uterine pedicles. The infundibulopelvic ligament is dissected from the ureter, clipped, and cut using an energy device.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the ureter, avoiding thermal injury, and enters the uterine tunnel. The uterine artery is delineated and clipped securely. Cordal dissection reveals the deep uterine vein on the left side, which is also clipped. The left lateral dissection is completed, and the surgeon proceeds with anterior dissection, separating the bladder from the anterior surface of the uterus and vagina, meticulously defining both ureters up to the vesico-ureteric junction.

The surgeon dissects the vesico-vaginal plane between the bladder and the vagina, staying close to the anterior surface of the vagina and cervix. Small blood vessels at the vesico-ureteric junction are controlled using clips and an energy device. A McCartney's tube is used to perform an anterior colpotomy, ensuring both vesico-ureteric junctions remain visible throughout the procedure.

During the posterior colpotomy, the surgeon carefully dissects the rectovaginal septum while maintaining proximity to the posterior vaginal wall. The rectovaginal septum is delineated, and the rectal tube is safeguarded. The vagina is dissected from the rectal tube to ensure adequate length for colpotomy closure and future rectal stapling. A per rectal examination confirms the tumor's extent, and the rectal tube is stapled off using an endostapler, with care taken to protect both ureters. The specimen is delivered through a left flank incision.
The surgeon closes the vaginal vault using a barbed interlocking suture, ensuring the integrity and safety of both ureters during the procedure.

The surgeon performs a stapled colorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler after confirming the position of the colonic conduit. An air leak test is conducted to ensure the integrity of the anastomosis. A diversion loop ileostomy is created.
